segments.s641-g9: This chunk places the direct object (سپینود) before the verb and marks it with the object marker را. جفت follows to indicate the role 'pair/companion/mate' with بهرامشاه as its governor, so the whole phrase reads 'Sepinud (object) — a companion/mate of Bahramshah.' Word order is object + noun phrase (object marker + noun + apposition).
segments.s641-g10: سپردم is the speaker's perfect/preterite (or a past narrative) 'I entrusted/handed over.' بدین is the locative phrase 'to/into this' (here split in the annotation as ب + دین; the annotation's 'دین' senses do not fit the usage). نامور modifies پیشگاه ('renowned presence/court'), so the phrase attaches as a prepositional/complement 'to this renowned presence.'
segments.s641-g11: This is an optative formula: شهنشاه (the shah) followed by تا جاودان 'until/for ever' and the optative زنده‌باد 'may he live.' تا here expresses duration ('until/for'), and زنده‌باد is the fixed wish/benediction form.
segments.s641-g12: بزرگان همه 'all the great ones' is the subject phrase; پیش او 'before him/in his presence' gives the frame, and بنده باد is another optative formula meaning 'may (they) be servants.' باد is the optative particle 'may.' Word order is noun phrase + locative phrase + optative predicate.
segments.s641-g13: چو من بگذرم is a temporal clause 'when I pass' (چو = 'when'); بگذرم is a present/subjunctive narrative form 'I pass' or 'I shall pass.' زین here functions as 'from this' (annotated as زین but not the literal 'saddle'), qualifying سپنجی سرای 'spongy house/abode.' The clause sets the time condition for the following main clause.
segments.s641-g14: This is the main clause: به قنوج 'to Kannauj' is a directional or goal phrase, followed by بهرامشاهست (a fused predicate identifying the addressee/agent as Bahramshah) and رای 'decision/opinion/plan' as the noun meaning 'the counsel/decision is that...'. Word order is goal + predicate + noun indicating the decision.
segments.s641-g15: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/by' (ز = از), so ز فرمان این تاجور means 'by the command of this merchant.' مگذرید is a negative imperative (2nd person plural) 'do not let (it/him) pass' or 'do not spare.' Together this issues a command founded on someone else's order.
segments.s641-g16: تن مرده را is the definite object 'the dead body' (تن = body, مرده = dead, را = object marker). سوی آتش 'toward the fire' is a directional phrase, and برید (from بردن) here is an imperative 'carry/take' (2nd person plural). The clause is a direct command: 'carry the corpse to the fire.'
segments.s641-g33: به اندازه یارانش را هم‌چنین is a fronted noun phrase marked by را (the definite/object marker). به اندازه = 'to the measure/extent' and یارانش = 'his companions' (یاران + ـش 'his'). هم‌چنین = 'likewise/similarly' modifies the action that follows. Together this phrase means 'likewise, to the same measure as his companions' and is treated syntactically as the (definite/specified) target/degree of the ensuing verb.
segments.s641-g34: بیاراست اسپان به دیبای چین is the verb phrase. بیاراست is a past-tense/transitive verb 'adorned' (bi- + ārast), اسپان is the object 'the horses'. به here functions instrumentally ('with/by') and دیبای چین is the material/garment: 'brocade (dība) of China' (i.e. Chinese brocade). Word order is poetic (object and adverbial preposed), but verb governs the whole action.
segments.s641-g35: گسی کردشان شاد و خشنود شاه: کردشان here is a fused verb+object (کرد + شان) meaning 'made them'. گسی ('a certain' / an indefinite particle) introduces or highlights the action; شاد و خشنود are predicative adjectives 'happy and pleased'. شاه appears after the clause (poetic inversion) as the logical agent 'the king' who performed the action.
segments.s641-g36: سه منزل همی راند با او به راه: سه منزل = 'three stages' (منزل = stage of a journey). همی is the archaic/progressive particle often used in epic Persian to mark ongoing or habitual action; together with راند (past of راندن) it yields 'he rode/drove (on)'. با او = 'with him' and به راه = 'on the road/along the way'. Word order places the duration/extent (three stages) before the verb, as is common.
segments.s641-g37: نبد هم بدین هدیه همداستان: نبد is an archaic negative/past copular ('نبود' = 'there was not'). هم is an intensifier/‘even’ or 'also' here, and بدین (ب + دین in the tokenization) = 'to this/with this' (a demonstrative 'this'), هدیه = 'gift'. همداستان = 'of the same mind / in agreement'. The phrase means essentially 'there was no one not of one mind concerning this gift' i.e. 'no one opposed the gift / all agreed.'
segments.s641-g38: علف داد تا مرز هندوستان is a simple past clause: علف = 'fodder/grass' (the thing given), داد = 'gave/they gave' (past of دادن). تا here means 'as far as' (extent), مرز = 'border/frontier', هندوستان = 'Hindustan/India'. Literally 'they gave fodder as far as the border of Hindustan.'
segments.s641-g1: بیامد is a past-tense verb ('he came') placed at the clause start for poetic effect. ز میدان is an ablative prepositional phrase 'from the field/arena'. چو introduces a simile ('like/as') and links تیر ('arrow') with its origin phrase از کمان ('from the bow'), so the whole chunk reads 'he came from the field like an arrow from the bow.'
segments.s641-g2: This clause contains the verb رفت ('went') with its goal introduced by بر ('toward/to') and a possessive phrase دختر خویش ('his own daughter') whose possessive is split into خوی + ش. آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time/then'. Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis (preposition + object before the verb).
segments.s641-g3: قلم خواست is 'he desired/asked for a pen' with the verb خواست in past tense; the subject is omitted (typical in Persian). از ترک is a source phrase 'from the Turk' and the conjunction و links a parallel object phrase قرطاس خواست ('and he asked for paper'), repeating the verb for stylistic parallelism.
segments.s641-g4: ز is the preposition 'from'. The following sequence (مشک سیه سوده انقاس) is a compressed, poetic noun phrase of stacked modifiers before the final verb خواست; such dense compounding and some rare/archaic words occur in Ferdowsi. خواست again is the past verb 'he asked/wanted'. (Note: some items here are archaic or rare and are treated as fixed modifiers in a noun phrase.)
segments.s641-g5: سر عهد is an adverbial/nominal phrase 'at the head/start of the covenant/pledge'. کرد is the past auxiliary/verb 'did/made' and is followed by آفرین meaning 'praise/acclamation' (آفرین کرد = 'he praised' / 'he gave acclaim'). از نخست is a temporal modifier 'from the first' or 'from the beginning'.
segments.s641-g6: This clause begins with بران کو, a compact demonstrative/relative unit meaning roughly 'upon that which' or 'thereon/where', with کو functioning like an older relative/locative. جهان is the subject 'the world', followed by the ablative phrase از نژندی ('from baseness/disgrace'), and بشست is the past verb 'washed'. Word order is subject – adjunct – verb, a normal pattern here.
segments.s641-g7: بگسترد is a past-tense verb 'he/it spread' (the prefix ب here is part of the classical verbal form). هم 'also' adds emphasis, and پاکی و راستی are the coordinated nouns 'purity and truth/uprightness' as the direct objects or subjects of the spreading action — poetic word order places the verb before its coordinated nouns.
segments.s641-g8: سوی دیو is a prepositional/directional phrase 'toward the demon'. شد is the past copula/verb 'became' or 'turned'. The coordinated nouns کژی و کاستی ('crookedness and deficiency') function as the subject or theme that 'became' directed toward the demon; the clause uses a slightly inverted order (direction + verb + subject) for poetic effect.
segments.s641-g25: بدان شد is a compound in which the prefix ب- attaches to دان (consider/know) and is completed by شد 'became' — together giving the sense 'came to be/decided (so)'. شهنشاه (the subject) follows this verbal phrase, and همداستان is a predicate adjective meaning 'of like mind' (adjectives follow the noun). The whole segment therefore means 'the shah became/was of the same mind (about that)'.
segments.s641-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). او is the third‑person subject 'he'. بازگردد is an older/formal present/subjunctive form (‑د ending) used here to report a decided action or intention: 'should/ought to return' or simply 'return'. به + هندوستان marks direction/place 'to India'.
segments.s641-g27: ز (poetic از) means 'from/out of' and introduces a partitive expression. چیزی is an indefinite 'something/whatever'; که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'; باشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' meaning 'exists/is' here; به ایران‌زمین gives the location 'in the realm of Iran' (به used here with a locative sense). The phrase therefore means 'from whatever there was in the land of Iran'.
segments.s641-g28: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he commanded/ordered'. تا here introduces the purpose/result clause ('so that/that'). کرد is the verb 'do/make' (here part of the construction expressing the action to be taken). موبد is the object 'mōbed' (Zoroastrian priest). گزین is an older present/imperative form meaning 'choose'—together the subordinate clause reads 'that (they) choose a mōbed' or 'that a mōbed be chosen'.
segments.s641-g29: This is an enumerative phrase introduced by ز ('from/of'). دینار is 'dinar' (coin/money). The second item is introduced again by ز before گوهر شاهوار; گوهر is 'jewel/gem' and شاهوار is an adjective 'kingly/regal' modifying گوهر (adjective follows noun). The whole segment lists riches: 'dinars and regal jewels.'
segments.s641-g30: Again introduced by ز, تیغ is 'sword'. The coordinated ز خود and then و کمر بی‌شمار continue the list: خود here names another category of possessions (literally 'self/one's own' in the lexicon) and کمر بی‌شمار is 'countless girdles/belts' (noun کمر followed by the adjective بی‌شمار). The segment is a simple series of items, noun + adjective following the noun.
segments.s641-g31: ز دیبا و از جامه repeats the partitive 'from/of' with two materials: دیبا 'brocade' and جامه 'garment/robe' (here introduced by از, a variant of ز). نابسود is an adjective modifying جامه; in context it conveys something like 'unworn/unfading' or 'inexhaustible' (in the lexicon it appears as 'fruitless/to no avail' but here functions adjectivally to emphasize abundance/durability).
segments.s641-g32: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/which'. آن را is the direct object 'it' (آن) with the object marker را. شمار 'number/count' and کرانه 'edge/boundary' are coordinated by و. نبود is the past negative of بود, here meaning 'there was not'. The clause therefore states 'for it there was no number nor boundary' — i.e., it was innumerable and limitless.
segments.s641-g17: سپارید is an archaic/past verbal form (classical Persian past) meaning 'entrusted/placed' with the verb before its object (typical epic word order). گنجم is the thing entrusted (the object, here 'my treasure' with a possessive -م implied in the surface form) and the prepositional phrase به بهرامشاه marks the recipient/destination. The whole clause therefore reads 'he entrusted my treasure to Bahramshah.'
segments.s641-g18: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same' modifying a coordinated list of nouns. The nouns کشور، تاج، گاه، سپاه are linked by و in a standard coordinate series; گاه here is a poetic/older word for a seat or place of rule (rendered 'throne/seat' in the translation) and functions as one item in the list of things entrusted.
segments.s641-g19: This line uses an inverted/poetic object order: سپینود را places the recipient first with the object-marker را, and داد is the past verb 'gave.' منشور هند ('the Indian charter') is the thing given. In classical Persian narrative the recipient can be marked with را and placed before the verb for emphasis, producing 'he gave Sepinoud the Indian charter.'
segments.s641-g20: This is a descriptive noun phrase modifying منشور: نوشته (a participial adjective 'written') followed by the compound descriptor خطی هندوی ('in handwritten/Hindavi script'). The prepositional phrase بر پرند ('on/attached to a bird') indicates the medium or means (here likely a message fastened to a bird). Word order is adjective(s) after the head noun, a common classical arrangement.
segments.s641-g21: The clause begins with the locative phrase به ایران ('in/into Iran'), then the habitual/past-state marker همی بود ('was/remained' — classical imperfective/past), followed by the subject/name شنگل and the duration دو ماه placed after it. Classical Persian allows flexible order, so the verb precedes or separates the subject and duration.
segments.s641-g22: فرستاد is a past tense verb 'sent' (third-person past). پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards' linking the action to what preceded it. مهتری is the person sent (the direct object, here without an overt را — a common omission in poetry) and نزد شاه is the prepositional phrase 'to the presence/of the king' indicating the recipient of the envoy.
segments.s641-g23: به دستوری is an adverbial phrase 'by/under an order' (instrumental/causal). بازگشتن is the infinitive/nominal verbal form 'to return' used here as the action that follows the order, and به جای marks the destination 'to the place/post.' The infinitive after an adverbial like به دستوری expresses the commanded action.
segments.s641-g24: خود is a reflexive/possessive 'his own' (referring back to the subject), joined by و to a coordinated noun phrase نامداران فرخنده‌رای — 'the renowned (نامداران) qualified by the adjective فرخنده‌رای (auspicious/well-disposed).' The adjective follows the noun as is normal in Persian, and the whole phrase completes the destination started by به جای.

sentences.s641-s5: I entrusted Sepinud as a companion to Bahramshah, before this renowned court.
sentences.s641-s6: May the shah live forever; may all the great men be his servants before him.
sentences.s641-s7: When I pass from this spongy house, the decision is for Bahramshah to go to Kannauj.
sentences.s641-s8: By the order of this merchant do not let him go; carry the dead body toward the fire.
sentences.s641-s17: He likewise adorned the horses with Chinese brocade to the same measure as his companions.
sentences.s641-s18: The king made them merry and content; for three stages he rode along the road with him.
sentences.s641-s19: There was no one opposed to this gift; they furnished fodder as far as the frontier of Hindustan.
sentences.s641-s1: He came from the field like an arrow from the bow; at that time he went toward his daughter.
sentences.s641-s2: He asked for a pen from the Turk and asked for paper; he asked for (ink) from black musk and sought anqas.
sentences.s641-s3: At the head of the pledge he gave praise from the first; upon that — where the world washed itself of baseness.
sentences.s641-s4: Purity and truth also spread; crookedness and deficiency turned toward the demon.
sentences.s641-s13: The shah likewise came to be of the same mind — that he should return to India.
sentences.s641-s14: He ordered that, out of whatever there was in the Iranian land, a mōbed be chosen.
sentences.s641-s15: From dinars and from regal jewels, from swords and from possessions and countless girdles.
sentences.s641-s16: Of brocade and of unfailing garments — for those there was no count nor limit.
sentences.s641-s9: He entrusted my treasure to Bahramshah — that same realm, crown, throne (seat) and army.
sentences.s641-s10: He gave Sepinoud the Indian charter, written in the Hindavi script and placed on a bird (i.e. attached to a messenger).
sentences.s641-s11: Shangul remained in Iran for two months; then he sent (or dispatched) Mehtari to the king.
sentences.s641-s12: By order, (he) return(ed) to his post and to the famed, auspicious notables.
